notes: Phase 2a reopens nine previously-disputed connective claims in the Huss origin-story cluster (D22): the pre-Hus persecution preamble, two maternal-piety/blessing anecdotes, and the five-part 'two English strangers and their pictures' vignette the chapter uses to explain Huss's turn toward Wycliffe. All nine rule improbable as written, but framing gaps of 2-3 on six of them show genuine underlying phenomena survive at a weakened, general level (real Bohemian persecution and burning-for-heresy, real Wycliffite transmission from England via other named channels, real Hussite antithesis imagery, Huss's own real attachment to such imagery) - these are absoluteness/retrojection failures over authentic substrates, not inventions from nothing, echoing the anachronistic pattern Phase 1 already found in this chapter's connective tissue. None is load-bearing: the chapter's documentary spine (Huss's and Jerome's trial, letters, and execution) is untouched, and the one causal claim that most directly touches the central continuity thesis (GC-06-032, Huss's turn toward Wycliffe) is redundant with an independently well-supported causal path already given in paragraph 6 (Jerome's imported writings, Queen Anne's circulation). GC-06-032 scores highest in the cluster as the fact/frame-tied causal hinge; the motive-attribution failure (GC-06-013) and the two devotional set-pieces (GC-06-013, GC-06-015) score lowest as purely decorative color; GC-06-031 is the sole total failure with no recoverable weakened version at all, though its narrative weight remains low.

GC-06-009.reasoning: A fact failure: the specific staging (forest/mountain worship, a blanket 'all who departed shall be burned' decree) is improbable, while the weakened core - real, documented pre-Hus Bohemian persecution, with burning as the actual legal penalty for obstinate/relapsed heresy - rises to well-supported, making this an absoluteness/retrojection failure over a genuine substrate rather than an invented event. Centrality is moderate: the claim opens the chapter's persecuted-remnant preamble linking Waldenses to Huss and touches the book's continuity chain, but it is not load-bearing - delete it and the chapter's own argument (Huss as a documented persecuted reformer) is untouched, since the trial/execution narrative that follows carries the chapter regardless.

GC-06-013.reasoning: A motive-attribution failure, distinct from the fact-failures in this cluster: the claim asserts a specific inner valuation ('education and the fear of God as the most valuable of possessions') for an unnamed, undocumented woman, while the one attested family-motive datum (Hus's own admission he first sought priesthood for money and prestige) points the other way. The weakened version reaches only genuinely-open - modern specialists are themselves split on whether the mother had any formative role at all - so an unresolved possibility survives, not vindication. Centrality is minimal: this is decorative biographical color establishing a pious-upbringing trope; deleting it changes nothing about the chapter's argument concerning Huss's reform career or martyrdom.

GC-06-015.reasoning: A fact failure: the specific devotional scene (widowed mother accompanying Huss to Prague and kneeling to bless him) has zero medieval attestation and traces to the Comenius-via-Wylie martyrological layer shared across this cluster. Even the generalized weakened version ('mother supported and blessed his departure') reaches only genuinely-open, meaning the softened claim remains an unresolved possibility rather than an established fact. As pure sentimental scene-setting it carries no weight in the chapter's actual argument about Huss's reform and trial; deleting it leaves the chapter's substantive claims entirely intact.

GC-06-027.reasoning: A fact failure: no attestation surfaced anywhere in the English, Czech, or German record for two English visitors arriving in Prague to spread Wycliffism; the episode traces to the same Comenius (1648) martyrological line feeding this whole cluster. The weakened version - Wycliffite teaching reaching Prague via people traveling from England - is well-supported through entirely different, documented channels (Jerome of Prague, Faulfis and Knehnic, Wyche, Payne), so the failure is retrojection of a plausible general phenomenon onto invented specific actors. As written this is the founding premise of a five-paragraph invented vignette (027-032); centrality is moderate rather than load-bearing because the chapter already establishes Huss's turn toward Wycliffe independently in paragraph 6 (Jerome's imported writings, Queen Anne's circulation), so deleting the whole vignette leaves that causal claim intact through its other supports.

GC-06-028.reasoning: A fact failure dependent on the same unattested episode as GC-06-027: the specific disputation-and-suppression scene involving the two strangers has no record, though the weakened, depersonalized claim (Wycliffite challenges to papal authority in Prague were suppressed, advocacy turning to indirect means) is well-supported by independently documented events (the 1403 condemnation of Wyclif's articles, the 1408 Black Rose disputation, 1410 book-burnings) - a rhetoric/absoluteness failure attaching a real institutional pattern to fictional actors. Centrality is moderate, tied to the same connective vignette as 027; it adds texture to the chapter's account of Bohemian resistance but nothing in the chapter's own trial/martyrdom argument depends on it.

GC-06-029.reasoning: A fact failure with an unusual twist: the act of these two named strangers publicly displaying the pictures is unattested, but the pictorial content itself - Christ meek on an ass versus the pope in triple crown on a mounted procession - is a genuinely documented Hussite propaganda motif (Nicholas of Dresden's Tabule, the Bethlehem Chapel murals Hus himself referenced, the Jena Codex), only misattributed roughly a decade early and to the wrong actors, so the weakened version reaches well-supported. Centrality is real rather than merely decorative: this motif recurs later in the chapter (paragraph 29, Huss's own documented prison dream about the Bethlehem Christ-pictures), giving it more narrative weight than a throwaway detail, though the chapter's argument about Huss's martyrdom does not depend on when or by whom the antithesis imagery first appeared.

GC-06-030.reasoning: A fact failure and the weakest evidentiary link among the picture-episode claims: the audience for an undocumented display is, as the ruling notes, 'a fortiori undocumented,' and the 'all classes' universal has no independent basis of its own. The weakened, generalized claim about broad social reach for Hussite antithesis propaganda reaches only probable, resting on inference from separate evidence (Nicholas of Dresden's Tables, the 1408 disputation crowd). Centrality is low: crowd reaction is a minor embellishment within an already non-central vignette, and deleting it affects nothing in the chapter's substantive argument.

GC-06-031.reasoning: A fact failure that is the most complete failure in this cluster: unlike its neighbors, no weakened version survives at all (ruling_weakened: n/a) - even a generalized 'the display provoked commotion and endangered its makers' claim has no independent correlate, since the one comparable documented case (Nicholas of Dresden's later exile and reported burning) belongs to different, later, named persons and cannot rehabilitate this scene. This is pure narrative closure for an already-fabricated legend, with nothing left standing even at a softened reading. Centrality remains low despite the total evidentiary failure, because the scene only closes out a vignette that is itself dispensable to the chapter's argument about Huss and Jerome's documented fate.

GC-06-032.reasoning: The failing components are tied between fact and frame (prioritized as fact per the charter), but the frame failure is substantively the important one: the claim that the pictures caused Huss's 'closer study of the Bible and of Wycliffe's writings' assigns a fictional cause to a real effect, since Huss's documented engagement with Wyclif (manuscript copies from 1398) predates the legend's 1404 setting and is better explained by university study and Jerome's imports. The weakened version reaches only probable - Huss genuinely valued and later deployed Christ/pope imagery at Bethlehem Chapel, attested in his own Constance prison letter - but that supports influence running the other direction, not this specific causal origin story. Centrality is the highest in this cluster: this is the hinge claim connecting the invented picture-vignette to Huss's real documented reform trajectory, most directly touching the continuity-of-truth-transmission thesis; it is still not load-bearing, because paragraph 6 already establishes Huss's independent turn toward Wycliffe's works through Jerome and Queen Anne, so the chapter's argument survives this claim's removal.
